{
  "isFinal": true,

    {
      "chunk": "Links:\n1. [docs/engineering/grill-with-docs.md](https://github.com/mattpocock/skills/blob/main/docs/engineering/grill-with-docs.md)\n2. [Claude Code skill: grill-with-docs — Agent Cookbooks](https://agentcookbooks.com/skills/grill-with-docs/)\n3. [Medium](https://medium.com/@anmjawad007/the-two-matt-pocock-skills-i-use-in-almost-every-ai-coding-session-57c468938145)\n4. [engineering/grill-with-docs/skills/grill-with-docs/SKILL.md at main · alirezarezvani/claude-skills](https://github.com/alirezarezvani/claude-skills/blob/main/engineering/grill-with-docs/skills/grill-with-docs/SKILL.md)\n5. [The /domain-modeling Skill](https://www.aihero.dev/skills-domain-modeling)\n\nSynthesis:\nGrill-with-docs is an AI coding skill that interviews you to sharpen a plan or design, grounding the conversation in your project's existing domain language and codebase [1][2][3]. It functions by performing a one-question-at-a-time, relentless interrogation that challenges vague terminology and tests edge cases [4][2][5].\n\nThe skill maintains state by writing directly to your repository [1][6]:\n\n* CONTEXT.md: Stores resolved terminology as a canonical glossary [4][5].\n* docs/adr/: Stores architectural decisions, but only when they are hard to reverse, surprising, and the result of a clear trade-off [4][7].\n\nUse it when you want to stress-test a plan against your project's current vocabulary [2][3]. If you lack a codebase, use the stateless grill-me; if you only need to manage terminology or ADRs without a full design interview, use the domain-modeling skill [1][6][8].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003edocs/engineering/grill-with-docs.md\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://github.com/mattpocock/skills/blob/main/docs/engineering/grill-with-docs.md\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e&#96;grill-with-docs&#96; interviews you relentlessly about a plan or design, one question at a time, until you and the agent reach a shared understanding — and it writes the vocabulary and decisions down as you go.\n...\nReach for it at the very start of a change, when the plan is still fuzzy and the domain language isn't settled, and you want to stress-test both before any code exists. If you only want the interview and don't need the artifacts, use [grilling](https://aihero.dev/skills-grilling); if the plan is already clear and you just need to pin down or record terminology, use [domain-modeling](https://aihero.dev/skills-domain-modeling). And if the change is too big to hold in one session and its route is still foggy — a greenfield project, a huge feature build — start upstream with [wayfinder](https://aihero.dev/skills-wayfinder): it charts the effort as a map of decisions, then hands back to this main flow once the way is clear.\n...\nThis skill is stateful — it writes into your repo as it grills. Resolved terms land in a &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; glossary at the root (or the relevant context's &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; if a &#96;CONTEXT-MAP.md&#96; marks a multi-context repo), and genuinely hard-to-reverse decisions land as ADRs under &#96;docs/adr/&#96;. Both are created lazily — nothing exists until the first term or decision crystallises — so you don't need to scaffold anything up front, but you do need to be somewhere it's safe to write these files.\n...\nWhat makes this variant its own skill is where the answers go. As the grill runs, fuzzy language gets sharpened into canonical terms and written to the glossary inline — not batched at the end. The glossary stays a glossary: pure vocabulary, no implementation details, no spec. ADRs are offered sparingly, only when a decision is hard to reverse, surprising without context, and the result of a real trade-off. Most sessions produce a sharper glossary and few or no ADRs, and that's the intended shape.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eClaude Code skill: grill-with-docs — Agent Cookbooks\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://agentcookbooks.com/skills/grill-with-docs/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e\u003e An interactive Claude Code skill that interrogates a plan against the project's existing domain model — its glossary, prior decisions, and architecture decision records — sharpening terminology and updating CONTEXT.md or BRAND.md inline as terms get resolved.\n...\n&#96;grill-with-docs&#96; is the project-aware sibling of &#96;grill-me&#96;. Where &#96;grill-me&#96; interrogates a plan in isolation, &#96;grill-with-docs&#96; interrogates it against the documents your project already has — domain glossary, decision records, brand book, CLAUDE.md, whatever's there. Each question is grounded in something the project already says.\n...\n: sharper plans plus a continuously-updated CONTEXT.md (or BRAND.md, or whichever doc you're maintaining) that absorbs new terminology as the session resolves it.\n...\n- Working in a project that already has a glossary, ADR folder, or CONTEXT.md\n- A new plan that uses overloaded terminology (e.g., \"user,\" \"session,\" \"agent\") where consistency matters\n- Onboarding a feature into an existing domain — you don't want to redefine concepts that already have names\n- Whenever drift in vocabulary between docs and code is becoming a real cost\n...\n1. You state the plan. Sloppy is fine.\n2. The skill scans the project's docs — CONTEXT.md, BRAND.md, decision records, root README — and indexes the terminology.\n...\n3. It picks one branch and asks one question, citing the doc. \"Your plan uses 'session' but CONTEXT.md defines it as a 30-minute window. Did you mean that, or something else?\" — narrow, grounded, sharp.\n...\n4. As you answer, the skill updates the doc inline to absorb new terms or refine existing definitions. By the end of a session, your plan is sharper and your CONTEXT.md is more accurate than it was an hour ago.\n...\n- Catches semantic drift between plan\n...\nand project glossary\n...\nmetastasizes\n- Forces precision on overloaded terms (\n...\n've been using interchangeably for months)\n...\nside-effect that compounds:\n...\nsession leaves the docs slightly better\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eMedium\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://medium.com/@anmjawad007/the-two-matt-pocock-skills-i-use-in-almost-every-ai-coding-session-57c468938145\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eSoftware projects live or die by how well you communicate intent. When you work with coding agents such as Claude Code, alignment is everything. Matt Pocock’s skills repository contains two tools that address the alignment problem from different angles: grill‑with‑docs and handoff. I’ve been using both in my daily workflow, and the combination has reshaped how I plan, code and document.\n...\nAt its core,&#96;grill‑with‑docs&#96; is an interview mechanism. The skill runs a structured grilling session that challenges every aspect of a proposed change. The spec defines it as a grilling session that challenges your plan against the existing domain model, sharpens terminology and updates documentation (&#96;CONTEXT.md&#96;, ADRs) inline as decisions crystallize. In other words, it doesn’t just ask questions; it forces you to anchor answers in the language of your project.\n...\n- Searches for an existing&#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; file and reads the glossary for the current bounded context. If your repository uses multiple contexts, it follows a&#96;CONTEXT‑MAP.md&#96; to locate the right glossary.\n...\n- Challenges your language against that glossary, flags conflicts and proposes precise canonical terms. When the session encounters vague or overloaded words, it asks clarifying questions and suggests alternatives such as “Customer” vs. “User”.\n...\n- Creates Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) only when needed. An ADR template is intentionally minimal — title and a sentence or two describing what we decided and why. You only create an ADR when the decision is hard to reverse, surprising without context and involves a real trade‑off.\n...\nSince adopting&#96;grill‑with‑docs&#96;, my planning sessions have become\n...\nbut more effective. I rarely need\n...\nrevisit earlier assumptions because the skill surfaces edge cases\n...\nnon‑obvious decisions—like using&#96;ON DELETE RESTRICT&#96; for pitches—so future changes don’t inadvertently undo deliberate\n...\n. The net effect is\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eengineering/grill-with-docs/skills/grill-with-docs/SKILL.md at main · alirezarezvani/claude-skills\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://github.com/alirezarezvani/claude-skills/blob/main/engineering/grill-with-docs/skills/grill-with-docs/SKILL.md\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003edescription: Docs-anchored grilling session — challenges a plan against the project's existing language (CONTEXT.md) and recorded decisions (docs/adr/), and updates those files inline as terminology and decisions crystallise. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan against documented domain language, or mentions \"grill with docs\".\nlicense: MIT\nmetadata:\n  derived_from: \"https://github.com/mattpocock/skills/tree/main/skills/engineering/grill-with-docs\"\n  original_author: \"Matt Pocock (@mattpocock)\"\n  original_license: MIT\n  voice: \"Matt Pocock — relentless, one-at-a-time, codebase-and-docs-first, ADRs only when 3 criteria are met\"\n  version: 1.0.0\n...\n\u003e Derived from [Matt Pocock's grill-with-docs](https://github.com/mattpocock/skills/tree/main/skills/engineering/grill-with-docs) (MIT, © 2026 Matt Pocock). Matt's interview discipline + docs-anchored grilling rules preserved verbatim under MIT. Additions in this repo: 3 stdlib validators (CONTEXT.md linter, ADR scanner, glossary↔code consistency check), 3 in-depth references each citing 7+ authoritative sources, &#96;cs-grill-with-docs&#96; agent, &#96;/cs:grill-with-docs&#96; command. See [Wrapper additions](#wrapper-additions) below.\n...\nterm is resolved. If\n...\nWhen the user uses a term that conflicts with the existing language in &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96;, call it out immediately. \"Your glossary defines 'cancellation' as X, but you seem to mean Y — which is it?\"\n...\n1. **Pre-flight (before the first question):**\n   - Run &#96;scripts/context_md_linter.py CONTEXT.md&#96; if a &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; exists — confirms the glossary is well-formed before grilling against it.\n   - Run &#96;scripts/adr_scanner.py docs/adr/&#96; if &#96;docs/adr/&#96; exists — surfaces numbering gaps, malformed ADRs, status-frontmatter inconsistencies.\n   - Run &#96;scripts/glossary_code_consistency.py --context CONTEXT.md --code src/&#96; — flags defined-but-unused terms (dead glossary) and code-only common nouns that may need definitions. Use these flags as opening grill questions.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eThe /domain-modeling Skill\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://www.aihero.dev/skills-domain-modeling\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e&#96;domain-modeling&#96; builds and sharpens a project's ubiquitous language as you design — challenging fuzzy terms, stress-testing relationships with concrete scenarios, and writing the glossary and decisions down the moment they crystallise.\n...\nReach for it when the words are the problem: two people mean different things by \"cancellation\", \"account\" is doing three jobs, or a design conversation keeps snagging on a concept that has never been named precisely. If instead the module's shape is the problem — where the seam goes, how deep the interface is — use codebase-design. If you want the plan itself interrogated before you build, use grilling.\n...\nThe skill writes into two places, both created lazily — only once there is something to record. Resolved terms go into &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; at the root (or, in a multi-context repo flagged by a &#96;CONTEXT-MAP.md&#96;, into the per-context &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96;). Decisions go into &#96;docs/adr/&#96;. Nothing needs to exist up front; the first resolved term creates the glossary, the first real trade-off creates the ADR.\n...\n- The glossary (&#96;CONTEXT.md&#96;) captures language. Every time a vague term is made canonical, it's written down inline — not batched — so the shared vocabulary stays current with the conversation. It stays ruthlessly free of implementation detail.\n- An ADR captures a decision, and the bar is high: offered only when the choice is hard to reverse, surprising without context, and the result of a real trade-off. Miss any one of the three and there is no ADR. This is what keeps &#96;docs/adr/&#96; a record of consequential forks rather than a diary.\n...\n&#96;domain-modeling&#96; is the single source of truth for building the project's ubiquitous language, split out as its own model-invoked skill so any other skill can reach it. grill-with-docs leans on it to record terms and decisions as a grilling session runs, triage uses it to keep tickets in the project's own words, and improve-codebase-architecture reaches for it while it works.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

{
  "markdown": "---\nname: domain-modeling\ndescription: Build and sharpen a project's domain model. Use when the user wants to pin down domain terminology or a ubiquitous language, record an architectural decision, or when another skill needs to maintain the domain model.\n---\n\n# Domain Modeling\n\nActively build and sharpen the project's domain model as you design. This is the *active* discipline — challenging terms, inventing edge-case scenarios, and writing the glossary and decisions down the moment they crystallise. (Merely *reading* &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; for vocabulary is not this skill — that's a one-line habit any skill can do. This skill is for when you're changing the model, not just consuming it.)\n\n## File structure\n\nMost repos have a single context:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/\n├── CONTEXT.md\n├── docs/\n│   └── adr/\n│       ├── 0001-event-sourced-orders.md\n│       └── 0002-postgres-for-write-model.md\n└── src/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf a &#96;CONTEXT-MAP.md&#96; exists at the root, the repo has multiple contexts. The map points to where each one lives:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/\n├── CONTEXT-MAP.md\n├── docs/\n│   └── adr/                          ← system-wide decisions\n├── src/\n│   ├── ordering/\n│   │   ├── CONTEXT.md\n│   │   └── docs/adr/                 ← context-specific decisions\n│   └── billing/\n│       ├── CONTEXT.md\n│       └── docs/adr/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nCreate files lazily — only when you have something to write. If no &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; exists, create one when the first term is resolved. If no &#96;docs/adr/&#96; exists, create it when the first ADR is needed.\n\n## During the session\n\n### Challenge against the glossary\n\nWhen the user uses a term that conflicts with the existing language in &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96;, call it out immediately. \"Your glossary defines 'cancellation' as X, but you seem to mean Y — which is it?\"\n\n### Sharpen fuzzy language\n\nWhen the user uses vague or overloaded terms, propose a precise canonical term. \"You're saying 'account' — do you mean the Customer or the User? Those are different things.\"\n\n### Discuss concrete scenarios\n\nWhen domain relationships are being discussed, stress-test them with specific scenarios. Invent scenarios that probe edge cases and force the user to be precise about the boundaries between concepts.\n\n### Cross-reference with code\n\nWhen the user states how something works, check whether the code agrees. If you find a contradiction, surface it: \"Your code cancels entire Orders, but you just said partial cancellation is possible — which is right?\"\n\n### Update CONTEXT.md inline\n\nWhen a term is resolved, update &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; right there. Don't batch these up — capture them as they happen. Use the format in [CONTEXT-FORMAT.md](./CONTEXT-FORMAT.md).\n\n&#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; should be totally devoid of implementation details. Do not treat &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; as a spec, a scratch pad, or a repository for implementation decisions. It is a glossary and nothing else.\n\n### Offer ADRs sparingly\n\nOnly offer to create an ADR when all three are true:\n\n1. **Hard to reverse** — the cost of changing your mind later is meaningful\n2. **Surprising without context** — a future reader will wonder \"why did they do it this way?\"\n3. **The result of a real trade-off** — there were genuine alternatives and you picked one for specific reasons\n\nIf any of the three is missing, skip the ADR. Use the format in [ADR-FORMAT.md](./ADR-FORMAT.md).\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mattpocock/skills/main/skills/engineering/domain-modeling/SKILL.md"
}

{
  "markdown": "# CONTEXT.md Format\n\n## Structure\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;md\n# {Context Name}\n\n{One or two sentence description of what this context is and why it exists.}\n\n## Language\n\n**Order**:\n{A one or two sentence description of the term}\n_Avoid_: Purchase, transaction\n\n**Invoice**:\nA request for payment sent to a customer after delivery.\n_Avoid_: Bill, payment request\n\n**Customer**:\nA person or organization that places orders.\n_Avoid_: Client, buyer, account\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Rules\n\n- **Be opinionated.** When multiple words exist for the same concept, pick the best one and list the others under &#96;_Avoid_&#96;.\n- **Keep definitions tight.** One or two sentences max. Define what it IS, not what it does.\n- **Only include terms specific to this project's context.** General programming concepts (timeouts, error types, utility patterns) don't belong even if the project uses them extensively. Before adding a term, ask: is this a concept unique to this context, or a general programming concept? Only the former belongs.\n- **Group terms under subheadings** when natural clusters emerge. If all terms belong to a single cohesive area, a flat list is fine.\n\n## Single vs multi-context repos\n\n**Single context (most repos):** One &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; at the repo root.\n\n**Multiple contexts:** A &#96;CONTEXT-MAP.md&#96; at the repo root lists the contexts, where they live, and how they relate to each other:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;md\n# Context Map\n\n## Contexts\n\n- [Ordering](./src/ordering/CONTEXT.md) — receives and tracks customer orders\n- [Billing](./src/billing/CONTEXT.md) — generates invoices and processes payments\n- [Fulfillment](./src/fulfillment/CONTEXT.md) — manages warehouse picking and shipping\n\n## Relationships\n\n- **Ordering → Fulfillment**: Ordering emits &#96;OrderPlaced&#96; events; Fulfillment consumes them to start picking\n- **Fulfillment → Billing**: Fulfillment emits &#96;ShipmentDispatched&#96; events; Billing consumes them to generate invoices\n- **Ordering ↔ Billing**: Shared types for &#96;CustomerId&#96; and &#96;Money&#96;\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe skill infers which structure applies:\n\n- If &#96;CONTEXT-MAP.md&#96; exists, read it to find contexts\n- If only a root &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; exists, single context\n- If neither exists, create a root &#96;CONTEXT.md&#96; lazily when the first term is resolved\n\nWhen multiple contexts exist, infer which one the current topic relates to. If unclear, ask.\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mattpocock/skills/main/skills/engineering/domain-modeling/CONTEXT-FORMAT.md"
}
```

{
  "markdown": "# ADR Format\n\nADRs live in &#96;docs/adr/&#96; and use sequential numbering: &#96;0001-slug.md&#96;, &#96;0002-slug.md&#96;, etc.\n\nCreate the &#96;docs/adr/&#96; directory lazily — only when the first ADR is needed.\n\n## Template\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;md\n# {Short title of the decision}\n\n{1-3 sentences: what's the context, what did we decide, and why.}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThat's it. An ADR can be a single paragraph. The value is in recording *that* a decision was made and *why* — not in filling out sections.\n\n## Optional sections\n\nOnly include these when they add genuine value. Most ADRs won't need them.\n\n- **Status** frontmatter (&#96;proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded by ADR-NNNN&#96;) — useful when decisions are revisited\n- **Considered Options** — only when the rejected alternatives are worth remembering\n- **Consequences** — only when non-obvious downstream effects need to be called out\n\n## Numbering\n\nScan &#96;docs/adr/&#96; for the highest existing number and increment by one.\n\n## When to offer an ADR\n\nAll three of these must be true:\n\n1. **Hard to reverse** — the cost of changing your mind later is meaningful\n2. **Surprising without context** — a future reader will look at the code and wonder \"why on earth did they do it this way?\"\n3. **The result of a real trade-off** — there were genuine alternatives and you picked one for specific reasons\n\nIf a decision is easy to reverse, skip it — you'll just reverse it. If it's not surprising, nobody will wonder why. If there was no real alternative, there's nothing to record beyond \"we did the obvious thing.\"\n\n### What qualifies\n\n- **Architectural shape.** \"We're using a monorepo.\" \"The write model is event-sourced, the read model is projected into Postgres.\"\n- **Integration patterns between contexts.** \"Ordering and Billing communicate via domain events, not synchronous HTTP.\"\n- **Technology choices that carry lock-in.** Database, message bus, auth provider, deployment target. Not every library — just the ones that would take a quarter to swap out.\n- **Boundary and scope decisions.** \"Customer data is owned by the Customer context; other contexts reference it by ID only.\" The explicit no-s are as valuable as the yes-s.\n- **Deliberate deviations from the obvious path.** \"We're using manual SQL instead of an ORM because X.\" Anything where a reasonable reader would assume the opposite. These stop the next engineer from \"fixing\" something that was deliberate.\n- **Constraints not visible in the code.** \"We can't use AWS because of compliance requirements.\" \"Response times must be under 200ms because of the partner API contract.\"\n- **Rejected alternatives when the rejection is non-obvious.** If you considered GraphQL and picked REST for subtle reasons, record it — otherwise someone will suggest GraphQL again in six months.\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mattpocock/skills/main/skills/engineering/domain-modeling/ADR-FORMAT.md"
}
```

**Why this next:** Voice is fallback-only, but `speak` still returns a boolean. The OpenSpec leaves the soft-fail policy to you: return `false`, or treat some cases as intentional success. Orchestration may log that value; Reveal must not depend on it.

**Options:**
1. **Always honest** — `false` if no audio played (`enabled: false`, missing key, API/playback failure).
2. **Disabled = success** — `true` when voice is intentionally off; `false` only when it was supposed to speak and couldn’t.
3. **Never alarm** — `true` for every soft failure so the pipeline always looks green.

**Recommendation:** Option 2 — disabled is a planned no-op (`true`); real failures (`false`) stay visible in logs without blocking Reveal.

**Question:** Which return policy do you want for `speak` when it’s disabled or fails?

**Why this next:** Your first 30 minutes need a real ElevenLabs call. Spec says config is local and gitignored, but not the exact shape on disk. That choice affects smoke-test setup, demo machine paste, and how orchestration/env docs stay clean.

**Options:**
1. **Env vars only** — e.g. `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`, voice ids, `VOICE_ENABLED`. Fast to paste; weak for the full routing table.
2. **Local JSON** (gitignored) matching §8.2 `VoiceConfig` — full routing table; needs a committed `.example` template.
3. **Hybrid** — JSON for routing/model; API key from env only.

**Recommendation:** Option 3 — keep secrets out of files people might commit by accident; keep the routing table editable without touching code.

**Why this next:** Hybrid needs a concrete file home for the smoke test and stub window. That also decides who can touch it (your folder only vs shared root).

**Options:**
1. **`voice/config.local.json`** (gitignored) + committed **`voice/config.example.json`** — stays in your seat; clear ownership.
2. **Repo-root shared config** (e.g. `.local/voice.json`) — easier for demo machine “one place for secrets-adjacent config,” but shared surface / shout-out risk.
3. **User home path** (outside the repo) — never committed; worse for teammates cloning the demo setup.

**Recommendation:** Option 1 — matches no-cross-edit and Person 6 ownership.
